title: Chicken noodle soup
subtitle: Homemade chicken broth with shredded leg meat and egg noodles.
excerpt: Chicken legs simmered slowly with onion, carrot, celery, and bay until the broth is golden and the meat falls from the bone. Shredded chicken and egg noodles go back in for the last few minutes. Better than anything from a tin.
sourceNotes: Chicken broth soups appear in almost every culinary tradition and in British cookbooks from at least the eighteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), includes a white chicken soup (entry 177) using the broth from a boiled fowl. The noodle version is American in popular association but draws on the same European broth-based tradition. Project Gutenberg #10136.

content.text: The difference between this and tinned chicken noodle soup is the broth. A broth made by simmering chicken legs for an hour is a different substance from reconstituted stock powder: golden, unctuously savoury, and full of gelatin from the bones. It takes patience but not skill.
Chicken legs rather than breast are the right choice. They have more flavour, more collagen, and survive the long cooking without drying out. The meat shreds easily after an hour in the broth.

items.name: Carrot
items.prepNote: two roughly chopped for stock, one diced finely for the soup

items.name: Celery
items.prepNote: two roughly chopped for stock, one diced for soup

content.text: A large, deep saucepan is the main kit. A ladle for skimming the foam. A sieve is useful if you want a very clear broth.

Place {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs in a large saucepan with the quartered onion, roughly chopped carrots and celery (reserve the diced pieces for later), {{garlic}} of bruised garlic, {{bay-leaves}} of bay, {{thyme}} of thyme, and {{black-pepper}} of whole peppercorns. Cover with {{water}} of cold water. Bring slowly to the boil over a medium heat, this takes about 15 minutes.

As the water comes to the boil, grey foam will rise to the surface. Skim it off with a ladle. Reduce the heat to a very gentle simmer and cook uncovered for 60 minutes. The broth should tremble, not boil.

Lift out the chicken pieces and set aside to cool for 10 minutes. Strain the broth through a sieve back into the cleaned pan, discarding the spent vegetables and aromatics. Return the broth to a simmer over medium heat. Add the reserved diced carrot and celery and cook for 8 minutes until tender. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt.

While the vegetables cook, pull the chicken meat from the bones and shred it into pieces. Discard the skin and bones. Add {{noodles-egg}} of egg noodles and the shredded chicken to the simmering broth. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes until the noodles are just tender. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve in deep bowls with chopped parsley.

items.fix: Cloudy broth is fine to eat; it just looks different. For a clearer broth, strain through muslin cloth next time and keep the simmer very gentle.
items.cause: Water boiled too hard during simmering, or foam was not skimmed at the start.

items.fix: Simmer the strained broth uncovered for 15 minutes to concentrate it before adding the noodles. A stock cube can also boost flavour in a pinch.
items.cause: Chicken pieces too small, or water too abundant for the amount of chicken.

items.fix: Add noodles in the last 3 to 4 minutes only. Serve immediately, noodles continue to soften in the hot broth even off the heat.
items.cause: Added too early, or cooked for too long.

items.fix: A gentle simmer, not a rolling boil, keeps chicken thigh meat moist. The meat should slide off the bone; if it does not, cook for another 10 minutes.
items.cause: Overcooked at too high a temperature.

content.text: Chicken noodle soup is one of the most widely eaten soups in the world. The principle is the same everywhere: a bird, water, aromatics, time. The noodle element brings it into the American domestic tradition, where it became synonymous with comfort eating and recovery from illness through the twentieth century. The homemade version is not faster than opening a tin, but it bears almost no resemblance to it in flavour.
